title: practical english usage - Part 266
content:
Wake me at ten past seven, (     .. . ten past seven o'clock.) Wake me at seven (o'clock).

The  expression o'clock is only used at the hour. Compare: three minutes to nine (More natural than three to nine.) seven minutes past eight (More natural than seven past eight.) divisions.

People generally prefer to say minutes past/to for times between the five-minute Americans prefer to write a colon between the hours and the minutes: 8:50.

8.50      eight fifty (   ten to nine) or 8.45      eight forty-five (   a quarter to nine) or 8.35   eight thirty-five (   twenty-five to nine) or 8.30      eight thirty (   half past eight) or 8.25   eight twenty-five (   twenty-five past eight) or 8.15   eight fifteen (   a quarter past eight) or 8.10      eight ten (   ten past eight) or 8.05   eight (oh) five (   five past eight) There are two common ways of saying what time it is.

1  saying     what    time    it  is 325 telling     the   time the Common Era’ ) and CE ('Common Era').

However, instead of BC and AD, many people now prefer to use BCE (‘ before The emperor Trajan was born in AD 53  / 53  AD.

Julius Caesar first came to Britain in 55  BC.

‘ in the year of the Lord’ ). BC follows the date; AD can come  before or after it.

the abbreviations BC (= Before Christ) and AD (= Anno Domini - Latin for To  distinguish between dates before and after the birth of Christ, we  use 3 BC and   AD or What date is the meeting? (   When is the meeting?) What's the date (today)? What date is it?

To  ask about dates, we  can say for instance: It's April (the) first.

To  announce the date, It's is used.

2005 = two thousand and five   twenty oh five 2000 =  two thousand 1946 = nineteen (hundred and) forty-six 1910  = nineteen (hundred and) ten 1498 = fourteen (hundred and) ninety-eight or 1305 = thirteen hundred and five   thirteen oh(ldu/) five 1200 = twelve hundred or thirtieth .. ,ɔ      ‘ the thirtieth of March, nineteen ninety-three’ 30  March  1993  = ‘ March  the thirtieth, nineteen ninety-three’  (AmE  also  'March 2 speaking Vocabulary Areas  Section 29 vocabulary •  326   names and titles: Daniel; Mr Lewis We're going to Ireland with Emily and Daniel Sinclair.

Isn't that Daniel Connolly the actor?

This is neutral - neither particularly formal nor particularly informal.

b  first name + surname (also last     name       or family       name) How's Mia getting on at school?

Where's Daniel? He said he'd be here at three.

friends and children.

We use first names mostly informally, for colleagues, relatives, a  first name (also Christian        name;      AmE   also given      name       or forename) When  we  talk (or write) about people we  can name them in four ways.

1  talking     about     people to them. There are some differences.

Names and titles are used both when talking about people and when talking 326 names     and   titles:   Daniel;     Mr   Lewis or 21.00 = nine o'clock in the evening (   nine pm) or 09.00 = nine o'clock in the morning (   nine am) ‘ before midday') and pm (= post meridiem - ‘ after midday').

evening. In a more formal style, we  can use am (= Latin ante meridiem - If necessary, times can be distinguished by using in the morning/afternoon/ Jack and I are meeting up at two o'clock.

- The meeting will begin at fourteen hundred.

‘ What time do you stop work? ‘ Half past five.' - The next train from Platform 5 is the 17.30 departure for Carlisle.

I'm seeing Oliver at a quarter past eight this evening.

- Last check-in time is 20.15.

official announcements. In ordinary speech, people usually use the twelve-hour The  twenty-four hour clock is used mainly in timetables, programmes and 3 the   twenty-four        hour    clock ‘ What time is it by your watch?') or What time do you make it? (   What do you make the time? BrE, meaning What's the time? Could you tell me the time? (more formal) What time is it? Have you got the time?
